class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Marketing and the Marketing Concept
learning_objectives: Define marketing and its role in creating customer value
Explain the marketing concept vs. production and sales orientations
Identify the components of the marketing mix (4 Ps)
readings: Kotler & Armstrong, Principles of Marketing Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Marketing is about understanding customer needs and creating value. This course covers the fundamental frameworks that drive marketing strategy and execution.
key_concepts: Marketing defined: creating, communicating, delivering value
Production, selling, and marketing orientations
The marketing mix: Product, Price, Place, Promotion
Customer value proposition
Marketing environment scanning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing how Nike's marketing strategy exemplifies the marketing concept across all 4 Ps
setup_requirements: Textbook: Kotler & Armstrong, Principles of Marketing (18th ed.)

review.value: Achieving organizational goals by identifying and satisfying customer needs better than competitors
Levitt's warning that companies fail when they define themselves by products rather than customer needs

learning_objectives: Identify psychological and social factors influencing buying behavior
Distinguish between qualitative and quantitative market research methods
readings: Kotler & Armstrong, Principles of Marketing Ch. 5-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding why consumers buy is the foundation of effective marketing. Market research provides the data needed to make informed marketing decisions.
key_concepts: Consumer decision process: need recognition through post-purchase
Psychological factors: motivation, perception, learning, attitudes
Social influences: reference groups, family, social class
Primary vs. secondary research
Surveys, focus groups, observational research, and experiments
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a market research plan for a new product launch, including survey instrument design
setup_requirements: Survey design tools (Google Forms or Qualtrics)

review.value: Need recognition, information search, evaluation of alternatives, purchase decision, post-purchase evaluation
Primary: collected firsthand for specific purpose; Secondary: already existing data collected for other purposes

learning_objectives: Apply segmentation bases: demographic, psychographic, behavioral, geographic
Evaluate market segments for targeting decisions
Develop a positioning statement and perceptual map
readings: Kotler & Armstrong, Principles of Marketing Ch. 7

lecture_notes.introduction: STP is the strategic heart of marketing. You cannot serve everyone equally well, so you must choose which customers to serve and how to differentiate yourself in their minds.
key_concepts: Market segmentation bases and variables
Segment evaluation: size, growth, structural attractiveness, fit
Targeting strategies: undifferentiated, differentiated, concentrated, micro
Positioning: identifying points of difference and parity
Perceptual mapping and positioning statements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a perceptual map of the smartphone market and crafting positioning statements for three brands

review.value: Segmentation divides the market, Targeting selects which segments to serve, Positioning establishes the brand's place in consumers' minds
For [target], [brand] is the [frame of reference] that [point of difference] because [reason to believe]

learning_objectives: Classify products and describe the product life cycle
Explain brand equity and brand management strategies
readings: Kotler & Armstrong, Principles of Marketing Ch. 8-9

lecture_notes.introduction: Products are more than physical items. They are bundles of benefits that include features, branding, packaging, and services that together create value for customers.
key_concepts: Three levels of product: core benefit, actual product, augmented product
Product life cycle: introduction, growth, maturity, decline
Brand equity: awareness, associations, perceived quality, loyalty
Brand extension and line extension strategies
New product development: idea generation through commercialization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Brand audit exercise comparing Coca-Cola and Pepsi's brand equity components

review.value: Marketing strategy must adapt at each stage: build awareness at intro, differentiate at growth, defend share at maturity, harvest or divest at decline
The commercial value derived from consumer perception of the brand name, beyond the functional product attributes

learning_objectives: Apply cost-based, value-based, and competition-based pricing methods
Evaluate channel conflict and management approaches
readings: Kotler & Armstrong, Principles of Marketing Ch. 10-12

lecture_notes.introduction: Price is the only marketing mix element that generates revenue. Distribution channels determine how and where customers access your product. Both must align with your positioning.
key_concepts: Cost-plus pricing vs. value-based pricing
Price elasticity of demand
Psychological pricing and price anchoring
Direct vs. indirect distribution channels
Intensive, selective, and exclusive distribution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Pricing simulation exercise: setting optimal price for a new product using demand curves and cost data

review.value: Set price based on perceived value to the customer rather than cost of production; requires deep customer insight
Must balance customer convenience (intensive) with brand exclusivity (selective/exclusive) and margin requirements

learning_objectives: Design an integrated marketing communications plan
Compare advertising, PR, sales promotion, and personal selling
Evaluate the impact of digital and social media on the promotion mix
readings: Kotler & Armstrong, Principles of Marketing Ch. 14-15

lecture_notes.introduction: Promotion is how you communicate your value proposition to target customers. Integrated marketing communications ensures all channels deliver a consistent message.
key_concepts: The communication process: encoding, channel, decoding, feedback
Advertising: reach, frequency, media planning
Public relations and earned media
Sales promotions: push vs. pull strategies
Digital marketing and social media integration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Developing an IMC plan for a product launch with budget allocation across channels
setup_requirements: Media rate cards and reach data

review.value: All communication channels must deliver a unified, consistent message that reinforces the brand's positioning
Attention, Interest, Desire, Action: the hierarchy of effects that promotion aims to move customers through

questions.question: A luxury watch brand is considering entering a new market segment of 'affordable luxury' priced at $200-$500. Using STP concepts, evaluate one major risk and one major opportunity of this targeting decision.
questions.answer: Risk: Brand dilution. Moving into affordable luxury could damage the brand's positioning among current high-end customers who associate the brand with exclusivity. The existing brand equity built on prestige may be undermined. Opportunity: Market expansion. The affordable luxury segment is growing rapidly, especially among millennials who aspire to luxury brands. This could dramatically increase volume and introduce new customers who may trade up to higher-priced lines over time.

rubric.key: Identifies a substantive risk with reasoning
Identifies a substantive opportunity with reasoning

questions.question: Explain the difference between demographic and psychographic segmentation, and give an example of when psychographic segmentation would be more useful than demographic.
questions.answer: Demographic segmentation divides markets by measurable characteristics like age, income, gender, and education. Psychographic segmentation divides by lifestyle, values, personality, and attitudes. Psychographic is more useful when demographics alone don't explain behavior. For example, marketing organic food: income and age alone don't predict who buys organic, but psychographic variables like health consciousness, environmental values, and lifestyle choices are strong predictors.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of both segmentation types
Valid example where psychographic is superior
Clear reasoning for why demographics fall short

questions.question: A software company currently charges $99/year for its product. A competitor launches a similar product at $49/year. Should the company match the price? Explain using pricing strategy concepts.
questions.answer: Not necessarily. The decision depends on the company's positioning and value proposition. If the company has strong brand equity and differentiated features, a value-based pricing strategy justifies the premium. Matching price signals that the products are interchangeable and triggers a price war that erodes margins. Instead, the company should reinforce its unique value through marketing and consider a tiered pricing structure that offers a basic version at a competitive price while maintaining premium pricing for the full product.

rubric.key: Recommends against automatic price matching with reasoning

questions.question: Explain why Apple uses a selective distribution strategy for its products rather than intensive distribution.
questions.answer: Apple uses selective distribution to maintain brand prestige and control the customer experience. By limiting sales to Apple Stores, authorized retailers, and its website, Apple ensures products are displayed and demonstrated properly, staff are trained, and the purchasing environment reflects the brand's premium positioning. Intensive distribution (selling everywhere) would undermine the luxury perception, reduce Apple's control over pricing and presentation, and potentially damage brand equity. Selective distribution also enables higher margins for retail partners, incentivizing them to invest in the Apple experience.

major_assignments.title: Marketing Plan Development
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive marketing plan for a new product or service entering a real market. The plan must include environmental analysis, STP strategy, complete marketing mix decisions, and an implementation timeline with budget.
objectives: Apply the complete marketing planning process
Conduct market research and competitive analysis
Develop an integrated marketing strategy across all 4 Ps
Create realistic implementation and budget plans
deliverables: STP strategy with positioning statement and perceptual map

criteria: Target market well-defined with data support
Comprehensive market analysis grounded in data
Coherent strategy across all marketing mix elements
Actionable implementation plan with realistic budget

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of marketing concepts and frameworks

class_participation.description: Active engagement in case discussions and group exercises

major_assignments.description: Marketing plan and brand analysis projects

midterm_examination.description: Covers marketing fundamentals, consumer behavior, and STP

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering full marketing mix and strategy

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Active participation in class discussions contributes to participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Marketing plan milestones penalized 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 4 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Group marketing plan is collaborative; individual quizzes and exams must be independent work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All research must be properly cited. Fabricating market data or copying plans from prior semesters is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to market research databases through university library. Presentation software for final marketing plan delivery.
